question:
Which modeling approach for RF power transistors is most commonly used in commercial CAD simulators and why is it preferred over purely physical or behavioral models?

A. Physical models are most common because they directly solve device physics and fabrication geometry, providing inherently accurate scaling for power transistors.
B. Equivalent circuit models are most common because they translate device physics into circuit elements, balancing empirical fitting and physical basis, making them practical for CAD simulation.
C. Behavioral models dominate because they use abstract equations fitted solely to terminal voltages and currents, allowing easy modeling of complex nonlinear effects without empirical data.
D. Purely physical and behavioral models are equally used because they each provide complementary advantages in modeling device nonlinearity and scaling for PA design.

detailed_sources.source: /home/jw161/chatbot/rag_textbook_paper_pdf/textbook_pdf/RF Power Amplifiers for Wireless Comunications - Steve C. Cripps.pdf
detailed_sources.text_snippet:
RF power bipolar junction transistors (BJTs) and metal oxide semiconductor field
effect transistors (MOSFETs) as it is for the more recent metal semiconductor FET
(MESFET) and heterojunction bipolar transistor (HBT) devices, although the latter
seem to catch most of the attention in technical journals and symposia.
Attempts to model devices for CAD applications fall into three general
categories:
 Physical models, where the device physics and fabrication geometries are
modeled from the “bottom-up”;
 Equivalent circuit models, where the device physics is translated into analo-
gous circuit elements;
 Behavioral models, where an essentially abstract set of equations is fitted to
the measured three-terminal DC characteristics.
Generally speaking, most CAD device models for RF applications use the sec-
ond category, with some additional help from either the first or the third. The BJT
modeling community has, historically, used the physical (“bottom-up”) model

cepts driving the technological RFMW evolution. These concepts are utilized later for 
the design of amplifiers, mixers, oscillators, and other circuits developed in subsequent 
chapters. Our approach intends to enable the reader to formulate and develop his or her 
own network descriptions as part of an integrated strategy to construct suitable models 
of analog RF circuits. 
Before developing appropriate network models for active devices, a short discus- 
sion of solid-state physics involving pn and metal-semiconductor junctions is 
presented. The aim is to provide a solid-state perspective of the electric circuit repre- 
sentations derived from the physical device level. This is needed because 
at high-frequency modes of operation, additional capacitive and inductive effects 
enter the solid-state devices and affect their performance 
the high-frequency behavior of many active devices markedly departs from that of 
low-frequency components and therefore requires special treatment

question:
In a Class D half-bridge amplifier operating above resonance, the turn-off switching loss consists of power losses during the voltage rise time \( t_r \) and the current fall time \( t_f \). Given that the drain-to-source voltage during rise time can be approximated as a parabolic function \( v_{DS2} = \frac{V_I (\omega t)^2}{(\omega t_r)^2} \) and the switch current during rise time can be approximated as a constant \( I_{OFF} \), while during fall time the switch current decreases linearly and the voltage is constant at \( V_I \), which expression correctly represents the total turn-off switching loss \( P_{toff} \) at switching frequency \( f \)?  

A. \( P_{toff} = f V_I I_{OFF} \left( \frac{t_r}{2} + \frac{t_f}{3} \right) \)  

B. \( P_{toff} = f V_I I_{OFF} \left( \frac{t_r}{3} + \frac{t_f}{2} \right) \)  

C. \( P_{toff} = f V_I I_{OFF} \left( \frac{t_r}{4} + \frac{t_f}{4} \right) \)  

D. \( P_{toff} = f V_I I_{OFF} \left( \frac{t_r + t_f}{2} \right) \)

question:
In an RF power amplifier operating near but not exactly at the resonant frequency f_0, the instantaneous drain-to-source voltage and current have a phase difference \( \phi \). How does this phase difference \( \phi \) affect the average drain power delivered to the load, and what implication does this have for designing the amplifier's output matching network?

A. The average drain power is independent of \( \phi \), implying that the matching network design can ignore phase considerations.
B. The average drain power decreases proportionally to \( \cos \phi \), indicating that the matching network must minimize phase shift to maximize output power.
C. The average drain power increases with \( \cos \phi \), so introducing a large phase shift improves efficiency.
D. The average drain power varies with \( \sin \phi \), which requires designing the network to maximize sine of the phase difference.

question:
In a Class A RF power amplifier, why is the maximum drain efficiency limited to approximately 50% when using a radio frequency choke (RFC) load, and what implication does this have on the operating currents and voltages?

A. Because the transistor operates with a conduction angle of less than 180°, limiting peak current and voltage swings; thus, maximum efficiency occurs at half the peak current and voltage.

B. Because the transistor is biased at a dc current greater than the peak ac current, causing constant dc power regardless of output, with maximum efficiency at peak current and voltage equal to the dc values.

C. Because the RFC can be replaced by a quarter-wavelength transformer, which halves the output power and thus limits the efficiency to 50%.

D. Because power losses in parasitic resistances increase efficiency to 50%, which only occurs when the gate-to-source voltage equals the threshold voltage.

question:
How does the increase in operating frequency from 600 MHz to 60 GHz affect the achievable data rate in a microwave communication system, assuming a constant percentage bandwidth and binary phase shift keying modulation?

A. The data rate increases by a factor of 10 because the absolute bandwidth at 60 GHz is ten times larger than at 600 MHz.

B. The data rate remains the same since the percentage bandwidth is constant, so data capacity does not change with frequency.

C. The data rate decreases due to higher path loss and line-of-sight limitations at 60 GHz despite the larger absolute bandwidth.

D. The data rate increases linearly with frequency because antenna gain decreases, requiring higher modulation complexity.

question:
Question: In a feedforward linearization system, how does an increase in AM-PM distortion at a given input amplitude affect the Error Power Amplifier (EPA) correction signal compared to gain compression alone, and why is this significant in PA design?

A. Increasing AM-PM distortion reduces the EPA correction signal amplitude because phase errors cancel gain compression effects, simplifying the EPA design.

B. Increasing AM-PM distortion increases the EPA correction signal amplitude, typically requiring more EPA power than gain compression alone, thus placing a heavier workload on the EPA and impacting power budget calculations.

C. AM-PM distortion does not affect the EPA correction signal amplitude since the correction only compensates for gain compression, making the phase distortion irrelevant to EPA power requirements.

D. Increasing AM-PM distortion decreases the EPA correction signal amplitude but increases the phase noise, which indirectly affects the EPA power consumption through increased error vector magnitude.

question:
In the analysis of a common-gate amplifier stage, neglecting channel-length modulation and body effect, the input reflection coefficient \( S_{11} \) is derived as  
\[
S_{11} = \frac{Z_{in} - R_S}{Z_{in} + R_S} = \frac{1 - g_m R_S - C_{X} s}{1 + g_m R_S + C_{X} s}
\]  
where \( Z_{in} = \frac{1}{g_m} \parallel \frac{1}{j \omega C_X} \) and \( C_X = C_{GS} + C_{SB} \).  

Which of the following statements correctly describes how increasing the transconductance \( g_m \) affects the input matching as represented by \( S_{11} \)?  

A. Increasing \( g_m \) always improves input matching by reducing the magnitude of \( S_{11} \) toward zero because it lowers the input impedance \( Z_{in} \) closer to \( R_S \).  

B. Increasing \( g_m \) always worsens input matching because it increases the term \( g_m R_S \) in the denominator, raising the magnitude of \( S_{11} \).  

C. Increasing \( g_m \) improves input matching only at low frequencies where capacitive reactance \( \frac{1}{\omega C_X} \) is large, making \( Z_{in} \) dominated by \( \frac{1}{g_m} \), thus bringing \( Z_{in} \) closer to \( R_S \).  

D. Increasing \( g_m \) has no effect on input matching because \( S_{11} \) depends solely on the ratio of \( Z_{in} \) and \( R_S \), which are independent of \( g_m \).

question:
In analyzing a nonlinear dynamic system with a two-tone input, why does the output typically contain components at frequencies such as \( \omega_1 \pm \omega_2 \) and \( 2\omega_1 \pm \omega_2 \) rather than only at the original input frequencies \( \omega_1 \) and \( \omega_2 \)?

A. Because the system’s differential equation linearly combines only the input frequencies without generating new frequency components.
B. Because dynamic nonlinearities cause mixing of the input tones, generating harmonics and intermodulation products at sums and differences of the input frequencies.
C. Because the weakly nonlinear assumption limits the output frequencies strictly to the original input frequencies.
D. Because capacitive elements filter out the original frequencies leaving only the higher-order harmonics in the output.

beam interception and RF heating severely limits the peak
and average power that can be achieved at millimeter-wave
frequencies [3]. For example, theW -band coupled-cavity TWT
amplifier, the conventional device with the highest average
power capability at millimeter-wave frequencies, is limited to

the gold standard of P A linearization, struggle with linearizing
both in-band and out-of-band distortion [3].
In the analog domain, distortion reduction techniques in-
clude using a filter at the output of a dual-band P A that passes
the fundamental frequencies of each band while rejecting
distortion frequencies. While this technique is straightforward,
particularly in cases with wide spacing in frequency between
the fundamental and distortion frequencies, the pass-band loss
of such a filter degrades the system’s gain and efficiency and
must be traded off against stop-band rejection [4].
As an alternative analog approach, we propose an interstage

illustrated in Fig. 1. The filter’s purpose is not simply to
minimize the distortion product tones at the input of the final-
stage P A. Rather, it applies an appropriate amplitude and phase

13.5 Mixers 637
13.5 MIXERS
A mixer is a three-port device that uses a nonlinear or time-varying element to achieve
frequency conversion. As introduced in Section 11.1, an ideal mixer produces an output
consisting of the sum and difference frequencies of its two input signals. Operation of
practical RF and microwave mixers is usually based on the nonlinearity provided by either
a diode or a transistor. As we have seen, a nonlinear component can generate a wide variety
of harmonics and other products of input frequencies, so filtering must be used to select
the desired frequency components. Modern microwave systems typically use several mix-
ers and filters to perform the functions of frequency up-conversion and down-conversion
between baseband signal frequencies and RF carrier frequencies.
We begin by discussing some of the important characteristics of mixers, such as image

As an alternative analog approach, we propose an interstage network between the driver amplifier and final-stage P A, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The filter’s purpose is not simply to minimize the distortion product tones at the input of the final- stage P A. Rather, it applies an appropriate amplitude and phase

As introduced in Section 11.1, an ideal mixer produces an output consisting of the sum and difference frequencies of its two input signals. Operation of practical RF and microwave mixers is usually based on the nonlinearity provided by either a diode or a transistor. As we have seen, a nonlinear component can generate a wide variety of harmonics and other products of input frequencies, so filtering must be used to select the desired frequency components. Modern microwave systems typically use several mix- ers and filters to perform the functions of frequency up-conversion and down-conversion between baseband signal frequencies and RF carrier frequencies. A. \(\displaystyle \omega_o \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{L (C_1 + C_2)}}\) without dependence on \(g_m\), \(R_L\), or \(r_o\)

D. \(\displaystyle \omega_o \approx \sqrt{\frac{g_m}{C_1 C_2 R_L}}\), indicating the frequency is primarily controlled by the transconductance and load resistance

question:
In a charge pump PLL loop filter augmented with a capacitor \(C_2\) connected from the control voltage line to ground, what is the primary reason that \(C_2\) cannot be made arbitrarily large despite its effectiveness in reducing control voltage ripple?

A. Increasing \(C_2\) raises the current-to-voltage conversion impedance, increasing the amplitude of ripple voltage pulses.

B. Adding a large \(C_2\) introduces an additional low-frequency zero that destabilizes the loop by increasing phase lag.

C. A large \(C_2\) forms an additional pole \((R_1 C_{eq})^{-1}\) that reduces the phase margin and can degrade loop stability.

D. Because \(C_2\) directly increases the charge pump current \(I_p\), which can saturate the loop filter components.

11.3 QUANTIZATION NOISE REDUCTION TECHNIQUES
As explained in Section 11.2.4, the sharp rise in the quantization noise of/Sigma1/Delta1modulators
leads to substantial phase noise at the output of fractional-N synthesizers. In fact, this
phase noise contribution can well exceed that of the VCO itself. This issue is ameliorated
by reducing the PLL bandwidth, but at the cost of the advantages envisioned for fractional-
N operation, namely, fast settling and suppression of the VCO phase noise across a large
bandwidth. In this section, we study a number of techniques that lower the/Sigma1/Delta1modulator
phase noise contribution without reducing the synthesizer’s loop bandwidth.
11.3.1 DAC Feedforward

question:
In a magnetically saturated ferrite biased along the z-axis, the permeability tensor exhibits off-diagonal terms that imply which of the following physical phenomena?

A. An applied AC magnetic field component along x induces magnetization only along x with no phase shift.

B. The permeability tensor is symmetric with no imaginary components, indicating no gyrotropic behavior.

C. An applied AC magnetic field component along x induces a magnetization component along y that is phase shifted by 90 degrees.

D. Reversing the bias field changes both the diagonal and off-diagonal elements of the permeability tensor symmetrically.

in one of two states, where M =± Mr , giving a digital phase shift. The amount of differ-
ential phase shift between these two states is controlled by the length of the ferrite toroid.

question:
For a series connection of a resistor \( R \) and an inductor \( L \) with characteristic impedance \( Z_0 \), the normalized impedance is given by \( z_{in}(\omega) = r + j \omega L / Z_0 \), where \( r = R / Z_0 \). Conversely, for a series connection of a resistor \( R \) and a capacitor \( C \), the normalized impedance is \( z_{in}(\omega) = r - j / (\omega C Z_0) \). Based on these expressions and their representation on the Z-Smith Chart, what is the key difference in the location of the impedance points for the RL and RC series circuits on the Smith Chart?

A. Both RL and RC series impedances lie on the upper half of the Z-Smith Chart because reactance is always positive.
B. RL series impedances lie on the upper half and RC series impedances lie on the lower half of the Z-Smith Chart due to the sign of their normalized reactances.
C. Both RL and RC series impedances lie on the lower half of the Z-Smith Chart because they represent passive components.
D. RL series impedances lie on the lower half and RC series on the upper half of the Z-Smith Chart because inductors behave like capacitors at high frequencies.

question:
When modeling high-power power amplifiers (PAs) in multicarrier communication systems, why does using two-carrier dynamic measurement data rather than single-carrier static data improve simulation accuracy?

A. Because two-carrier measurements inherently eliminate all memory and hysteresis effects in the PA.

B. Because two-carrier dynamic data capture nonlinear interactions and memory effects more effectively, reducing their impact on simulation.

C. Because single-carrier static data are sufficient for capturing broadband modulation but not narrowband modulation characteristics.

D. Because using two-carrier data simplifies the simulation by ignoring modulation schemes and focusing only on power consumption.

detailed_sources.text_snippet:
polynomial coefficients in the determination of harmonic frequency compo-
nents. There are, nevertheless, some limitations to this approach. In particu-
lar, amplifiers which have substantial nonlinearity at the low end of their
power range as well as the high end do pose serious problems. Class B, or
deep Class AB PAs, clearly can exhibit this behavior to some extent.
While recognizing that this intermediate position will run into prob-
lems with some amplifier types, it should also be acknowledged that the use-
ful range of the technique is highly dependent on the manner in which the
polynomial coefficients, and the truncation of the power series, are derived.
One of the proposals in this chapter is that more focused effort should be
directed at using two-carrier tests to derive polynomial models rather than
single-carrier gain and phase sweeps. A simple two-carrier test at the peak
power level of a PA can give a firm indication as to how many degrees of dis-

But it sets a target and compiles a recipe for practical predistorter design.
Case 3: Unmatched PD
Clearly, there is wide scope for illustrating the effects of PDs which have
unmatched, or not perfectly matched, characteristics to the PA. Such cases
result in notched IM responses, such as shown already in Figure 5.5. As
discussed previously, IM notches at specific power levels are still possible,
although inclusion of AM-PM effects in the PA and PD characteristics tends
to reduce the depth of the notches in IM power sweeps. There is also a gray
area between cases 2 and 3, where the PD characteristics are close but
not precisely equal to the ideal values. Such cases represent many practical
situations.
The above cases have been analyzed using a simple two-carrier signal
environment. Before drawing too many conclusions about the results and

question:
In microstrip MIC directional couplers, the Lange coupler achieves higher coupling by splitting each transmission line into multiple interdigitated sections. Given that octave band 3-dB couplers require gaps around 25 microns and line widths between 25 and 50 microns for four interdigitated fingers, what is the primary reason this structure can achieve coupling factors as low as 1.5 dB compared to standard edge-coupled microstrip couplers?

A. The increased number of interdigitated sections effectively multiplies the coupling length, enhancing electromagnetic interaction.
B. The use of plated vias in the Lange coupler reduces radiation losses which improves coupling efficiency.
C. The coplanar waveguide configuration of the Lange coupler minimizes mixed dielectric effects, leading to better coupling.
D. The enlarged wirebond pads in the Lange coupler significantly reduce parasitic capacitance, allowing lower coupling factor.

B. Circuit Design of MMIC2 
Fig. 6 shows a block diagram of MMIC2. MMIC2 was 
designed by synthesizing two MMIC1 units, combining and 
dividing them using Lange couplers. The matching 
impedances of efficiency an d output power are generally 
different in high frequency PAs. Lange couplers can transform 
these impedances into the same impedance [7]. Additionally, 
Lange couplers reduce the exte rnal impedance fluctuations 
caused by wire bonds and achieve a good return loss (RL). Fig. 
7 shows a schematic and the simulation results of Lange 
coupler. The Lange coupler consists of two metal layers and 
an air bridge, and we perf ormed electromagnetic field 
calculations that take coupling into account. A fine layout with 
a line width of 5 μm, a line gap of 5 μm, and a line length of 
1440 μm is required to achieve a target coupling factor of 3 dB. 
The designed Lange coupler showed good results with an 
insertion loss (IL) of 3.4–3.5 dB, including GSG wire-bonding

ple 7.4.
7.5 THE QUADRATURE (90◦) HYBRID
Quadrature hybrids are 3 dB directional couplers with a 90 ◦ phase difference in the out-
puts of the through and coupled arms. This type of hybrid is often made in microstrip line
or stripline form as shown in Figure 7.21 and is also known as a branch-line hybrid. Other
3 dB couplers, such as coupled line couplers or Lange couplers, can also be used as quadra-
ture couplers; these components will be discussed in later sections. Here we will analyze
the operation of the quadrature hybrid using an even-odd mode decomposition technique
similar to that used for the Wilkinson power divider.
With reference to Figure 7.21, the basic operation of the branch-line coupler is as

Appendlx G Couplers 61 9 
G.3 Lange Coupler 
Port 1 Port 2 
Port 4 Port 3 
Figure G-7 A 3 dB Lange coupler. 
A popular implementation of the quadrature hybid in microstrip line form is the 
so-called Lange coupler shown in Figure G-7 for a four-strip configuration. Additional 
variations involve six- and eight-strip realizations. The interdigital form of the micro- 
strips permits a very compact geometric size and provides for tight coupling. 
Typical coupling values range between -5 and -1 dB. By choosing the length of 
the microstrip elements appropriately, a very broadband realization of up to 40% band- 
width can be achieved. 
Further Reading 
P. Karmel, G. Colef, and R. Camisa, Introduction to Electromagnetic and Microwave 
Engineering, John Wiley, New York, 1998. 
J. Lange, "Interdigitated Stripline Quadrature Hybrid," ZEEE Trans. on MTI: Vol. 17, 
pp. 1150-1151,1969.

question:
According to the Manley-Rowe relations for a lossless nonlinear reactive element driven by a single frequency source at angular frequency \(\omega_1\), if only the \(n\)th harmonic is delivered to a load through lossless reactive terminations at all other harmonics, what is the theoretical maximum conversion efficiency from the fundamental frequency \(\omega_1\) to the \(n\)th harmonic?

A. The efficiency is limited to \(\frac{1}{n^2}\) due to energy conservation constraints.

B. The maximum efficiency is \(100\%\), implying \(\left|\frac{P_{n0}}{P_{10}}\right|=1\).

C. The maximum efficiency is limited to \(50\%\) because of required idler currents at lower harmonics.

D. The efficiency cannot exceed \(20\log(n)\) dB due to noise multiplication effects.

A. \( R = \frac{V_{\mathrm{cc}}^{2}}{2 P_{\mathrm{out}}} \)

B. \( R = 1.365 \frac{V_{\mathrm{cc}}^{2}}{P_{\mathrm{out}}} \)

C. \( R = 0.732 \frac{P_{\mathrm{out}}}{\omega} \)

D. \( R = \frac{0.685}{\omega C} \)

question:
Which of the following design modifications would most effectively increase the transition frequency \( f_T \) of a microwave bipolar transistor, according to the provided analysis?

A. Increasing the emitter capacitance \( C_E \) while keeping the collector current \( I_C \) constant  
B. Increasing the base thickness \( d_B \) to reduce charge recombination effects  
C. Decreasing the base thickness \( d_B \) and increasing the base doping to minimize \( \tau_B \)  
D. Increasing the emitter resistance \( r_E \) by reducing the collector current \( I_C \)

question:
Given a transmission line with characteristic impedance \( R_{\mathrm{in}} = 50\,\Omega \) and a measured return loss of 20 dB, which of the following statements correctly describes the possible source resistances \( R_G \) and their relation to the reflection coefficient \(\Gamma_{\mathrm{in}}\)?

A. The unique source resistance is \( 61.1\,\Omega \) corresponding to \(\Gamma_{\mathrm{in}} = +0.1\), since the reflection coefficient cannot be negative.

B. Two possible source resistances exist, approximately \( 61.1\,\Omega \) and \( 40.9\,\Omega \), corresponding to reflection coefficients \(\Gamma_{\mathrm{in}} = +0.1\) and \(\Gamma_{\mathrm{in}} = -0.1\), respectively, because the return loss depends only on \(|\Gamma_{\mathrm{in}}|\).

C. Only one source resistance exists, \( 50\,\Omega \), because at 20 dB return loss the line is perfectly matched.

D. The source resistance can be any value between \(40.9\,\Omega\) and \(61.1\,\Omega\) because return loss defines a continuous range of \(\Gamma_{\mathrm{in}}\) values.

question:
In the simplified small-signal hybrid-π model of a bipolar transistor at room temperature, which relationship correctly expresses the input resistance \(r_{\pi}\) in terms of the forward DC current gain \(\beta_0\) and the transconductance \(g_m\), and why is this relationship significant for parameter extraction?

A. \(r_{\pi} = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m}\); because it links the input resistance directly to easily measurable DC and small-signal quantities, allowing straightforward extraction.

B. \(r_{\pi} = \frac{g_m}{\beta_0}\); because higher transconductance reduces the input resistance, simplifying the design of input matching networks.

C. \(r_{\pi} = \beta_0 \times g_m\); because the product of current gain and transconductance represents the overall input impedance in forward active mode.

D. \(r_{\pi} = \frac{1}{\beta_0 g_m}\); since it models the reciprocal relationship indicating the decrease in input resistance with increasing gain and transconductance.

question:
When extracting the intrinsic small-signal parameters of a MOSFET, why is it necessary to subtract the extrinsic parasitic capacitances \(C_{\mathrm{gp}}\) and \(C_{\mathrm{dp}}\) from the measured admittance \(Y\)-parameters before converting to impedance \(Z\)-parameters?

A. Because these capacitances cause a frequency-independent shift in the real part of the admittance, which would distort the intrinsic conductance values.

B. Because these capacitances contribute in parallel to the intrinsic capacitances, and must be removed to isolate the intrinsic device behavior accurately.

C. Because these capacitances appear only at very high frequencies, and subtraction filters out low-frequency noise components.

D. Because these capacitances are in series with the intrinsic capacitances, and subtracting them linearizes the admittance matrix for easier inversion.

question:
In designing a three-section input matching network for a bipolar transistor with an input impedance at center frequency \( f_c \) of \( Z_{\mathrm{in}} = (0.9 + j1.8) \,\Omega \), why is the device input lead inductance considered as the series inductive element of the first low-pass section, and what is the primary effect of including it in the matching network?

A. Because it is a large inductive reactance at \( f_c \), including it reduces the overall network \( Q \) by canceling capacitive reactance, thus broadening the bandwidth.

B. Because it represents unavoidable parasitic inductance at the device input, including it as the first section inductance allows for accurate compensation and minimizes quality factor \( Q \).

C. Because it can be treated as an ideal inductor, including it enhances harmonic suppression by increasing the network's selectivity and quality factor.

D. Because it is negligible compared to other reactances, including it simplifies the network design without significantly affecting bandwidth or gain ripple.

question:
What is the primary reason for avoiding the operation of power MOSFET switches below the resonant frequency \( f_o \) in a series-resonant circuit?

A. The output capacitance charging energy is doubled, causing excessive turn-on switching loss.
B. The transistor’s output capacitance cannot be fully discharged, leading to continuous conduction loss.
C. The diode antiparallel to the MOSFET experiences severe reverse-recovery current spikes that can damage the transistor.
D. The resonant inductor allows abrupt current changes, resulting in voltage spikes across the MOSFET during turn-off.

question:
In an ideal Class B push-pull amplifier with equal transformer turns and zero saturation voltage, the total DC collector current is given by the average of the sum of transistor collector currents, resulting in a DC power P_0 and an RF output power P_out. Considering these definitions, which of the following correctly explains why the maximum theoretical collector efficiency \(\eta = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%\) is achieved in this configuration?

A. Because the push-pull operation doubles the fundamental output voltage while maintaining the same DC power consumption, increasing the efficiency above 100%.
B. Because only one transistor conducts at a time during half a cycle, reducing the total DC current and thus power dissipation, while the output voltage remains sinusoidal.
C. Because the push-pull configuration cancels the odd harmonics in the output current, allowing all power to be delivered to the load without losses.
D. Because the input and output impedances increase by a factor of four, which inherently improves the efficiency of power transfer from DC supply to RF output.

The Kahn technique of envelope elimination and 
restoration (EEM) employs removes the amplitude 
modulation from the signal, sends the constant-magnitude 
signal through a high-efficiency power amplifier, and then 
replaces the amplitude modulati on into the DC supply of a 
second amplifier to re-modulate the signal at the output.  
This technique allows the main power amplifier to operate 
at a single amplitude, which is chosen to optimize the 
efficiency of the amplifier.   
The Doherty configuration is commonly used in an 
effort to increase efficiency for signals with large PAPR.  
The Doherty configuration is shown in Fig. 6.  It consists of 
a carrier amplifier designed in  a Class B configuration that 
allows the amplifier to operate within a reasonably high-
efficiency range for most of the signal.  A Class C peaking 
amplifier is also exposed to the input signal.  Because it is 
Class C, the peaking amplifier is biased below threshold, so

I. INTRODUCTION 
n amplifier is a device that increases primarily the voltage, 
current, or power of a given signal. They are widely used 
in wireless communications and broadcasting, and also in audio 
equipment. The basic principle of a power amplifier is 
converting the DC power drawn from a power supply to a 
desirable AC voltage signal and delivered to the load. An ideal 
power amplifier has an efficiency rating of 100% which shows 
that an input power is equal or the same to the output power. 
However, in reality some of the power is lost in the form of heat 
energy. Even the amplifier itself consumes undesirable amount 
of power during the amplification process. Most of the studies 
have shown that it consumes approximately 57% of all DC 
power when it is in maximum load. This means that the power

the traditional drawback of the class-C topology becomes an 
advantage for high energy efficiency. For enhancement-mode 
NMOS transistors where the threshold voltage, V
t, is positive, 
class-C operation occurs when the DC bias point, VBIAS, is set 
below the threshold voltage, Vt, as shown in Fig. 2. In normal 
operation, a voltage input signal with high amplitude is applied 
to the gate of the device such that it conducts drain current, i
D, 
only when the input signal is greater than Vt. The period of 
conduction relative to a complete cycle of the input signal is 
called the conduction angle, Φ. Because the PA conducts 
current only for a small fraction of the full cycle, the power 
consumption due to the overlapping of signal voltage and 
current in time is reduced; hence, the efficiency is increased 
relative to a traditional class-A linear amplifier, for example, 
that consumes bias current during the full cycle of the signal.

question:
Which statement correctly describes the nature and definition of the characteristic line impedance \( Z_0 \)?

A. \( Z_0 \) is the total impedance seen at the input of a transmission line, calculated using the total voltage and current at that point.
B. \( Z_0 \) is defined from the ratio of positive to negative traveling voltage waves and does not relate to current.
C. \( Z_0 \) is derived from the ratio of traveling voltage waves to their corresponding traveling current waves and is distinct from conventional circuit impedance defined by total voltage and current.
D. \( Z_0 \) is a purely real quantity representing the resistive losses in the transmission line independent of frequency.

question:
For a Class D RF power amplifier using a series-resonant network with loaded quality factor \( Q_L \), the 3-dB bandwidth \( BW \) is derived based on the voltage transfer function magnitude. Given the relationship  
\[
Q_L \left(\frac{f}{f_o} - \frac{f_o}{f}\right) = \pm 1,
\]
and that the upper and lower 3-dB frequencies are approximated as  
\[
f_H \approx f_o \left( 1 + \frac{1}{2 Q_L} \right), \quad f_L \approx f_o \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2 Q_L} \right),
\]
which of the following correctly explains how the 3-dB bandwidth \( BW = f_H - f_L \) depends on \( Q_L \) and the resonant frequency \( f_o \)?

A. The bandwidth \( BW \) is proportional to \( Q_L \) and increases as \( Q_L \) increases, given by \( BW = f_o Q_L \).

B. The bandwidth \( BW \) is inversely proportional to \( Q_L \) and decreases as \( Q_L \) increases, given by \( BW = \frac{f_o}{Q_L} \).

C. The bandwidth \( BW \) is independent of \( Q_L \) and constant at \( BW = 2 f_o \).

D. The bandwidth \( BW \) depends on the square of \( Q_L \) and is given approximately by \( BW = \frac{f_o}{Q_L^2} \).

question:
In a circular waveguide, the dominant TE\(_{11}\) mode propagates at a frequency just above its cutoff frequency, where \(k_c = \frac{p'_{11}}{a}\). Given that the propagation constant \(\beta = \sqrt{k^2 - k_c^2}\) and the wave impedance for the TE mode is \(Z_{TE} = \frac{\eta k}{\beta}\), which of the following statements correctly describes the behavior of the wave impedance \(Z_{TE}\) as the operating frequency approaches the cutoff frequency from above?

A. \(Z_{TE}\) decreases to zero because \(\beta \to k\) near cutoff, causing the denominator to increase.  
B. \(Z_{TE}\) increases without bound because \(\beta \to 0\) near cutoff, making the denominator approach zero.  
C. \(Z_{TE}\) remains constant at \(\eta\) regardless of frequency because it depends only on the intrinsic impedance.  
D. \(Z_{TE}\) approaches the intrinsic impedance of free space \(\eta\) as frequency approaches cutoff due to leveling of \(k\) and \(\beta\).

question:
In a practical resistor at room temperature, which of the following statements best explains why the rms thermal noise voltage increases with resistance?

A. Increasing resistance reduces the bandwidth over which noise is generated, thereby increasing voltage fluctuations.

B. Higher resistance causes more collisions among free electrons, increasing the random voltage fluctuations across the terminals.

C. Thermal noise voltage depends on the thermal agitation of charge carriers, and higher resistance converts this agitation into larger voltage fluctuations.

D. Increasing resistance increases the temperature of the resistor, which directly increases the rms noise voltage.

detailed_sources.text_snippet:
The characterization of noise effects in RF and microwave systems in terms of noise
temperature and noise figure will apply to all types of noise, regardless of the source, as
long as the spectrum of the noise is relatively flat over the bandwidth of the system. Noise
with a flat frequency spectrum is called white noise.
Noise Power and Equivalent Noise Temperature
Consider a resistor at a physical temperature ofT degrees kelvin (K), as depicted in Figure
10.2. The electrons in the resistor are in random motion, with a kinetic energy that is
proportional to the temperature. These random motions produce small, random voltage
fluctuations at the resistor terminals, as illustrated in Figure 10.2. This voltage has a zero
average value but a nonzero root mean square (rms) value given by Planck’s blackbody
radiation law,

question:
In a Class DE RF power amplifier operating at angular frequency \(\omega\), the loaded quality factor \(Q_L\) and the load resistance \(R\) are given. Considering the relations derived for the inductances \(L_a\) and \(L_b\) and the capacitance \(C\), which of the following expressions correctly describes the value of the capacitance \(C\) needed to satisfy the resonance condition \(\omega^2 = \frac{1}{L_a C}\)?

A. \(\displaystyle C = \frac{1}{\omega R (Q_L + \frac{\pi}{2})}\)

B. \(\displaystyle C = \frac{1}{\omega R \left(Q_L - \frac{\pi}{2}\right)}\)

C. \(\displaystyle C = \frac{R}{\omega (Q_L - \frac{\pi}{2})}\)

D. \(\displaystyle C = \frac{1}{\omega^2 R \left(Q_L - \frac{\pi}{2}\right)}\)

question:
In the common-drain Colpitts oscillator, the oscillation frequency is given by \(\omega_o = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}\). Considering the amplitude condition for steady-state oscillation \(g_m R = 1 + \frac{C_1}{C_2}\), what effect does increasing the capacitance \(C_2\) (while keeping \(C_1\) and \(L\) constant) have on the required transconductance \(g_m\) and the oscillation frequency \(\omega_o\)?

A. Increasing \(C_2\) decreases required \(g_m\) and increases \(\omega_o\).
B. Increasing \(C_2\) increases required \(g_m\) and decreases \(\omega_o\).
C. Increasing \(C_2\) decreases required \(g_m\) and decreases \(\omega_o\).
D. Increasing \(C_2\) increases required \(g_m\) and increases \(\omega_o\).

question:
When performing the DC characterization of a bipolar transistor in the forward active region, why is the base-collector junction typically short-circuited (i.e., \(V_{BC} = 0\)) during the measurement of forward current gain \(\beta_F\)?

A. To eliminate the influence of reverse current gain \(\beta_R\) by preventing injection across the base-collector junction  
B. To ensure that the Early effect is maximized, allowing accurate measurement of collector current variation  
C. To simplify the Ebers-Moll equations by removing the exponential term related to \(V_{BC}\), isolating the forward gain behavior  
D. To reduce the thermal voltage \(V_T\) influence on the base current, resulting in a more stable current gain measurement

question:
In a voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) using a tail-biased topology with AC-coupled varactors, increasing the coupling capacitor \(C_S\) to a value much larger than the maximum varactor capacitance \(C_{max}\) is done to minimize the series effect on tuning range. However, this introduces a parasitic capacitance \(C_b\) to ground. Considering \(C_S = 10C_{max}\) and \(C_b = 0.05C_S\), what is the primary consequence of these parasitic effects on the VCO's tuning range and oscillation frequency?

A. The tuning range increases because the parasitic \(C_b\) effectively adds to \(C_{var}\), shifting the frequency higher.
B. The tuning range decreases by approximately 10%, and the oscillation frequency decreases due to the added parasitic capacitance in parallel with \(C_1\).
C. There is no significant effect on tuning range or frequency because \(C_S\) is much larger than \(C_{max}\), effectively isolating the varactors.
D. The oscillation frequency becomes more sensitive to supply noise but the tuning range remains unchanged because the series capacitor blocks noise from the core.

switches have a width of 2μm forCu1 and Cu2 and 1.5μm forCu3–Cu6.
Second, to obtain a wide continuous tuning range, the gate of the varactor is capac-
itively coupled to the core and biased at Vb ≈ 0.6 V . As explained in Chapter 8, the
“bottom-plate” parasitic ofCc may limit the tuning range. Fortunately, however, our design
affords a large constant capacitance on each side, readily absorbing the parasitic ofCc. The
coupling capacitor can be realized with parallel plates [Figure 13.41(b)]. The value ofCc is
chosen about 10 times the maximum value of the varactor capacitance so as to negligibly
reduce the tuning range.

question:
Which of the following conditions must be satisfied to theoretically achieve 100% drain efficiency in a power amplifier using an NMOS transistor?

A. The product of instantaneous drain current and drain-to-source voltage must always be positive.
B. The instantaneous drain current and drain-to-source voltage waveforms must be nonoverlapping, so \( i_D v_{DS} = 0 \) at all times.
C. The transistor must be operated with a step change in drain-to-source voltage to maximize power dissipation.
D. The transistor capacitance \( C \) must be maximized to reduce power losses during switching transitions.

Fig. 9. Transient Waveforms of a Single MOSFET 
 
The peak drain efficiency is measured and calculated to be 
approximately 35% with an input power of 25dBm at 10GHz. 
The variation of the PAE versus the input power is shown in 
Fig. 10. The amplifier’s PAE is calculated taking into account 
the loss of all four switching PAs. As the input power is swept  
at 10GHz, the peak PAE is found to be 47%.

The class-F PA achieves high efficiency and high output 
power by using harmonic resonators in its output network to

fundamental and the first two harmonics since higher

8
Class F RF Power Amplifiers
8.1 Introduction
Class F radio-frequency (RF) power amplifiers [1–28] utilize multiple-harmonic resonators in
the output network to shape the drain-to-source voltage such that the transistor loss is reduced
and the efficiency is increased. These circuits are also called polyharmonic or multiresonant
power amplifiers. The drain current flows when the drain-to-source voltage is flat and low, and
the drain-to-source voltage is high when the drain current is zero. Therefore, the product of
the drain current and the drain-to-source voltage is low, reducing the power dissipation in the
transistor. This method of improving the efficiency is the oldest technique and was invented
by Tyler in 1919 [1]. Class F power amplifiers with lumped-element resonant circuits tuned
to the third harmonic or to the third and fifth harmonics have been widely used in high-power

question:
In designing a Class D half-bridge series-resonant amplifier operating below resonance, why is the drain–source capacitance \( C_{ds} \) at the dc supply voltage \( V_I \) significantly smaller than the zero-voltage junction capacitance \( C_{j0} \), and how does this affect the turn-on switching loss?

A. Because \( C_{ds}(V_I) \) decreases approximately as \( 1/\sqrt{V_I} \) due to junction voltage dependence, it reduces the energy stored and thus lowers turn-on switching loss compared to what \( C_{j0} \) suggests.

B. Because \( C_{ds}(V_I) \) is equal to the sum of \( C_{oss} \) and \( C_{rss} \), it is inherently larger than \( C_{j0} \), increasing the turn-on switching loss.

C. Because \( C_{ds}(V_I) \) increases linearly with \( V_I \), it causes the turn-on switching loss to rise sharply as the supply voltage increases, leading to inefficiency.

D. Because \( C_{ds}(V_I) \) is independent of the barrier potential \( V_B \), it remains constant with voltage, so turn-on switching loss is dominated by capacitance charging at zero voltage only.

A. The equivalent series resistance (ESR) of the physical inductor and capacitor  
B. The oxide breakdown of the gate oxide limiting voltage tolerance  
C. The switching dead time between the two transistor drive signals  
D. The body–drain intrinsic pn junction diode conduction threshold voltage

question:
Why are switching mode power amplifiers generally considered impractical at GHz frequencies in RF applications?

A. Because RF power transistors at GHz frequencies cannot be accurately modeled as simple switches due to their slow sweep through the linear region.

B. Because switching mode amplifiers have lower efficiency compared to Class B and Class F amplifiers at GHz frequencies.

C. Because the bandwidth of switching mode amplifiers is too narrow to handle GHz frequency signals effectively.

D. Because switching mode amplifiers require ideal switching elements that are readily available only above GHz frequencies.

detailed_sources.source: /home/jw161/chatbot/rag_textbook_paper_pdf/textbook_pdf/RF Power Amplifiers for Wireless Comunications - Steve C. Cripps.pdf
detailed_sources.text_snippet:
Probably the most well-known, and certainly most widely touted, switching
mode for RF applications is the Class E mode. Although in its most basic and origi-
nal form, a Class E device needs to have the same unreachable switching properties
as any other switching mode application, there appear to be derivatives of the classi-
cal switch-mode Class E PA which allow for slower switching but still appear to be
distinct from conventional PA modes. This metamorphosis is an important topic
and has been given a chapter to itself, Chapter 8; the present chapter will focus
mainly on the classical switching modes which can be used quite effectively below
1000 MHz.
The chapter starts by considering a simple switching device with a broadband
resistive load, and then considers the effect of tuning the load. Such an amplifier,
even with an ideal switching element, has surprisingly little to offer the RF designer
in comparison to the Class B or Class F modes considered in the previous chapters,
173

detailed_sources.text_snippet:
but serves as a useful introduction to the subject. The Class D amplifier is considered
next. In its ideal switching mode form, a “perfect” RF amplifier is created, with a
halfwave rectified sinewave of current and a squarewave of voltage. This produces
maximum possible fundamental RF power at 100% efficiency, but is difficult to
realize at higher RF frequencies.
The bulk of the chapter considers the classical Class E switching mode in some
detail. As a lead in to the following chapter, a simple design example illustrates how
in the “gray area,” at 800 MHz and using a GaAs MESFET device, a Class E ampli-
fier can be realized which uses a design procedure based almost entirely on the
results from the classical analysis.
Switching mode power amplifiers, without qualification, are highly nonlinear
devices. This reality leads to further rejection by the wireless communications indus-
try, on the basis that variable envelope signals cannot be passed without hopeless

When designing a high-power RF amplifier system for narrow-band operation, which type of power combiner is most suitable to ensure simple practical realization and effective power combination?
A. Coaxial cable combiner with ferrite cores  
B. N-way Wilkinson combiner  
C. Hybrid microstrip combiner  
D. Coaxial line transformer with impedance transformation

question:
Why is it generally not feasible to achieve a 50-Ω input resistance in an LNA by simply placing a physical resistor of 50 Ω in parallel at the input?

A. Because a 50-Ω resistor cannot provide a real impedance at high frequencies due to parasitic capacitances.
B. Because the resistor introduces additional thermal noise, increasing the noise figure above acceptable levels.
C. Because the resistor reduces the gain of the LNA to below unity, making it unusable.
D. Because the resistor creates excessive input reactance that cannot be canceled with inductors.

question:
In a Class B RF power amplifier using the square law MOSFET model, the gate-to-source voltage is \( v_{GS} = V_t + V_{gsm} \cos \omega t \) with \( V_{GS} = V_t \). If the saturation drain-to-source voltage is \( v_{DSsat} = V_{gsm} \), which design constraint on the minimum drain-to-source voltage \( v_{DSmin} \) and the maximum amplitude \( V_m \) of the drain-to-source voltage waveform ensures proper transistor operation without saturation degradation?

A. \( v_{DSmin} = V_{gsm} \) and \( V_m \leq V_I - V_{gsm} \) to keep the transistor operating near saturation without dropping below \( v_{DSsat} \).
B. \( v_{DSmin} = 0 \) and \( V_m = V_I \) so that the drain voltage swings fully from zero to supply voltage for maximum output power.
C. \( v_{DSmin} \) should be much less than \( V_{gsm} \) to allow increased voltage swing, and \( V_m \) can exceed \( V_I - V_{gsm} \) without affecting saturation.
D. \( v_{DSmin} \) must be equal to \( V_I \) and \( V_m = 0 \) to maintain constant drain voltage and avoid distortion.

question:
Question: In a push–pull Class E ZVS RF power amplifier with transformer turns ratio \( n \), how does the output power \( P_O \) scale with respect to the load resistance \( R_L \) and the turns ratio \( n \), and what is the physical reasoning behind this scaling?

A. \(P_O\) is proportional to \(\frac{V_I^2}{R_L}\) and independent of \(n\), because the transformer does not affect the output power.

B. \(P_O\) is proportional to \(\frac{V_I^2}{n^2 R_L}\), meaning the load resistance seen by each transistor is effectively scaled by \(n^2\), which increases output power by a factor of four compared to a single-transistor Class E amplifier.

C. \(P_O\) is proportional to \(\frac{V_I^2 n^2}{R_L}\), since the transformer increases voltage by \(n\), directly boosting output power by \(n^2\).

D. \(P_O\) is proportional to \(\frac{V_I^2}{n R_L}\), reflecting a linear scaling of load resistance with the transformer turns ratio, leading to doubled output power.

question:
In IS-95 CDMA, why is closed-loop power control necessary even after the initial open-loop power adjustment at the mobile unit?

A. Because the mobile unit’s transmit power must be increased continuously to overcome path loss growth.
B. Because the transmit and receive paths may experience different fading due to operating at different frequency bands.
C. Because the base station transmits many channels simultaneously requiring constant power level adjustments.
D. Because the spreading code rate changes dynamically, affecting the required power levels.

CDMA, power control is used in both the base-station transmitters and handset transmitters.

cell.Inhandsettransmitters,theoutputpowershouldbetransmittedatvariablelevelssothatthe
power levels of the signals received at the base station are similar to all users.

detailed_sources.text_snippet:
rate transmission) in mobile handsets have increased 
significantly. The additional active components demand 
higher current consumption which, inevitably, shortens the 
battery life and reduces talk time of the handsets. Hence, 
there is a constant need to reduce power consumption of 
the handset components in order to extend battery life. 
Power amplifier (PA) is one of the main current 
consuming parts within handsets. Traditionally, CDMA 
handset PAS are designed to meet stringent spectral re- 
growth requirement at maximum transmit power level. 
Statistically, however, handsets transmit at maximum 
linear output power only for a small fraction of time, and 
most of the transmission takes place at a considerably 
lower power level (over IOdB below maximum linear 
power) [l]. The power added efficiency (PAE) of the 
conventional PAS usually drop off rapidly under such 
backoff operation, resulting in higher current draw 
coupled with excessive linearity margin.

question:
In an Envelope Elimination and Restoration (EER) system, the mismatch in delay between the RF path and the low-frequency path causes intermodulation distortion (IMD), approximated by the formula
\[ IMD \approx 2 \pi BW_{\text{RF}}^2 \Delta \tau^2 \]
where \( BW_{\text{RF}} \) is the RF signal bandwidth and \( \Delta \tau \) is the delay mismatch. If the bandwidth is doubled, how does the IMD change assuming the delay mismatch remains constant?

A. IMD doubles because it is directly proportional to bandwidth.
B. IMD increases by a factor of 4 because it depends on the square of bandwidth.
C. IMD remains unchanged since bandwidth does not affect delay mismatch.
D. IMD increases by a factor of 8 because it scales with the cube of bandwidth.

question:
Which of the following statements correctly explains the relationship between drain efficiency (η) and power-added efficiency (PAE) in a common-source (CS) amplifier stage at different frequencies?

A. At low to moderate frequencies, the input power is significant, making PAE much lower than η.

B. At high frequencies, input capacitance reduces the real part of the input impedance, increasing PAE above η.

C. At low to moderate frequencies, the input impedance is capacitive with negligible average input power, so PAE approximately equals η.

D. Introducing a 50-Ω input resistance in a PA always makes PAE equal to η regardless of frequency.

splitting on the PAE of the DPA at backoff.

question:
Why is the peaking amplifier function considered a major challenge in realizing the full benefits of Doherty Power Amplifiers (DPAs) in modern multicarrier power amplifier (MCPA) systems?

A. Because the peaking amplifier requires excessive power consumption, making the overall system inefficient.

B. Because simple input bias offset methods cause many problems, necessitating more complex adaptive bias schemes that often reduce speed or limit implementation to the system level.

C. Because envelope detectors used in standalone power amplifiers cannot accurately measure the signal amplitude, preventing effective bias adaptation.

D. Because the transmitter cannot provide any information about the envelope amplitude of the signal, thus making bias adaptation impossible.

detailed_sources.source: /home/jw161/chatbot/rag_textbook_paper_pdf/paper_pdf/bulkdownload1/Advanced design methods of Doherty amplifier for wide bandwidth- high efficiency base station power amplifiers.pdf
detailed_sources.text_snippet:
A 2.14 GHz high power Doherty amplifier with wideband
and high power has been reported [1] - [4]. The reference [1]
delivers excellent efficiency performances, but have a problem
to improve the linearity over broad power levels. The problem
is caused by the undesired characteristics, such as the memory
effect related to wide bandwidth and incomplete modulation
due to the under-driving of the peaking amplifier. Usually, in a
conventional Doherty amplifier, the load impedances of both
cells cannot be fully modulated to the optimum impedance
and the peaking cell cannot generate full power because of
the class C bias. Thus, the bias adaptation technique is a
good approach to solve the problem. However, it increases the
complexity due to the external control circuitry and enhances
the memory effect [7], [8].
In this paper, we propose advanced design methods to
solve the memory effect for wideband signal and the under-
modulation problem of the conventional Doherty amplifier.

function of the input signal's envelope in order to reduce the 
DC power consumption of the amplifier and consequently 
improve its power efficiency. The advantage of such polar

question:
In the design of a basic Class E ZVS amplifier operating at duty cycle \( D = 0.5 \), the load resistance \( R \) is given by  
\[
R = \frac{8}{\pi^2 + 4} \frac{V_I^2}{P_O} \approx 0.5768 \frac{V_I^2}{P_O}
\]
If the amplifier is designed for a DC input voltage \( V_I = 100\, \mathrm{V} \) and output power \( P_O = 80\, \mathrm{W} \), what is the primary reason why the load resistance must be fixed at this exact value for the Class E basic resonant circuit?  

A. To ensure zero-voltage switching (ZVS) operation while allowing any load mismatch by adjusting \( C_1 \) accordingly.  

B. To achieve optimal Class E operation with the given input voltage and power since the basic resonant circuit does not provide impedance matching capability.  

C. To enable suboptimal operation with only partial ZVS and minimize power loss under short-circuit conditions.  

D. To maintain a constant output current amplitude independent of load power variations and thus reduce current ripple in the choke inductor.

question:
What is the primary advantage of feedforward linearization in microwave power amplifier design compared to other linearization techniques?

A. It reduces intermodulation distortion by at least 30 dB without being limited by signal bandwidth or video delay.

B. It significantly improves power-added efficiency by eliminating all memory effects in the power amplifier.

C. It is the most power-efficient linearization method and requires minimal gain and phase tracking adjustments.

D. It can only correct distortion in narrowband signals due to inherent video delay restrictions.

detailed_sources.text_snippet:
may be required as part of the design to eliminate harmonics and noise
power. Both amplifier chains would need tight temperature compensation,
but this is a likely specification requirement in any case.
6.10 Conclusions
The feedforward system is the mainstream technique for MCPA lineariza-
tion. With suitable gain and phase tracking controls, it is capable of reducing
the IM distortion of any PA by at least 30 dB, with no signal bandwidth or
video delay restrictions. Even PA memory effects, displayed on a modulation
domain timescale, can be fully corrected. In this sense the efficiency issue is
not a direct detraction, since no other system has been demonstrated with
comparable unrestricted linearization capability. It remains to be seen
whether this supremacy will soon be challenged by improving DSP-based
linearization systems which can potentially offer higher efficiency. The
power of feedforward should not be ignored by PA designers with more
modest linearization goals.

This paper aims to provide a design methodology which

Both amplifier chains would need tight temperature compensation, but this is a likely specification requirement in any case. 6.10 Conclusions The feedforward system is the mainstream technique for MCPA lineariza- tion. With suitable gain and phase tracking controls, it is capable of reducing the IM distortion of any PA by at least 30 dB, with no signal bandwidth or video delay restrictions. Even PA memory effects, displayed on a modulation domain timescale, can be fully corrected. In this sense the efficiency issue is not a direct detraction, since no other system has been demonstrated with comparable unrestricted linearization capability. It remains to be seen whether this supremacy will soon be challenged by improving DSP-based linearization systems which can potentially offer higher efficiency. The power of feedforward should not be ignored by PA designers with more modest linearization goals.

question:
In designing a two-port network with a single series admittance element \(Y\), why is it impossible to describe the network using \(Z\)-parameters, and which parameter sets provide a valid representation instead?

A. Because the series admittance network produces a non-singular \(Y\)-parameter matrix with zero determinant, making \(Z\)-parameters undefined; valid representations are given by \(H\)- and \(ABCD\)-parameters.

B. Because the series admittance network produces a singular \(Y\)-parameter matrix with zero determinant, making conversion to \(Z\)-parameters impossible; valid representations are given by \(H\)- and \(ABCD\)-parameters.

C. Because the series admittance network produces a singular \(Z\)-parameter matrix, making \(Y\)-parameters undefined; valid representations are given by only \(Z\)-parameters.

D. Because the series admittance network has equal off-diagonal \(Z\)-parameter elements preventing \(Y\)-parameter conversion; valid representations are given only by \(Z\)- and \(H\)-parameters.

since most of the material can readily be acquired in available communication systems 
engineering courses. 
The organization of this text is as follows: Chapter 1 presents a general explana- 
tion of why basic circuit theory breaks down as the operating frequency is increased to 
a level where the wavelength becomes comparable with the discrete circuit compo- 
nents. In Chapter 2 the transmission line theory is developed as a way to replace the 
low-frequency circuit models. Because of the voltage and current wave nature, Chap- 
ter 3 introduces the Smith Chart as a generic tool to deal with the impedance behavior 
on the basis of the reflection coefficient. Chapter 4 discusses two-port networks with 
their flow-chart representations and how they can be described on the basis of the so- 
called scattering parameters. These network models and their scattering parameter 
descriptions are utilized in Chapter 5 to develop passive RF filter configurations.

question:
According to the introduction on Class AB power amplifiers, what is a key limitation of classical theory when predicting the linearity of an RFPA operated below Class A bias?

A. It assumes the RF device can switch instantaneously, leading to overly optimistic linearity predictions.

B. It overestimates efficiency by ignoring the heatsink cooling improvements in Class AB operation.

C. It incorrectly models the device conduction as a perfect linear amplifier regardless of bias point.

D. It neglects the nonlinearity caused by thermal effects in the device under high power conditions.

and can easily override the intended effects of a classical Class AB design.
It has been suggested that a harmonic short circuit, placed as close to the device
input as possible, can restore the input voltage to the intended classical sinewave.
Although this may appear to be an analogous strategy for removing harmonics to
that which is mandatory at the output of a Class AB PA, the actual requirements at
the input are quite different. The harmonics generated by the varactor can in princi-
ple be manipulated to give better performance than reverting to sinusoidal excita -
tion. There is an additional issue, which is that a simple quarter-wave SCSS will
only short even harmonics. Figure 4.24 shows the effect of such an SCSS on the
waveforms of Figure 4.22, where the SCSS is placed at the closest accessible point
external to the device, which is the input of the gate resistance. Clearly, in this par-

detailed_sources.source: /home/jw161/chatbot/rag_textbook_paper_pdf/textbook_pdf/RF Power Amplifiers for Wireless Comunications - Steve C. Cripps.pdf
detailed_sources.text_snippet:
On the downside, peak voltages can be as high as three times the DC supply if
the power shortfall is to be kept within reasonable limits. A device with a given
breakdown voltage can usually be operated in Class AB at a supply voltage half of
this level. If a Class E design requires the voltage to be dropped to one third of the
breakdown level, it is only fair to take account of this in the PUF calculation, as dis-
cussed (but not implemented in Figure 7.13) in Section 7.5. There is also the issue of
power gain. If a typical Class E amplifier is running at 3–5 dB lower power gain, due
to the overdrive requirements on the input, than a comparable linear amplifier, the
upper frequency limit will be lower for a given technology. Generally speaking,
12–13 dB of linear gain would be required from a given device in order to stand a
chance of useful Class E operation; however, the same can be said of conventional
high efficiency modes with shorter conduction angles.

question:
In designing an integrated microstrip transmission line to minimize loss while maintaining a high characteristic impedance (Z₀), which of the following design changes would be most effective according to the discussed trade-offs?

A. Increasing the signal line width significantly to reduce series resistance, accepting a decreased Z₀ and thus lower inductance per unit length and higher capacitance.

B. Decreasing the thickness of the signal line metal layer to reduce surface roughness, maintaining the same line width to keep Z₀ constant.

C. Increasing the spacing between the signal line and the ground plane dielectric layers to lower capacitance and increase Z₀ without changing the signal line width.

D. Using polycrystalline silicon for the signal line instead of metal layers to reduce loss by lowering sheet resistance.

The loss of microstrips arises from the resistance of both the signal line and the ground
plane. In modern CMOS technologies, metal 1 is in fact thinner than the higher layers,
introducing a ground plane loss comparable to the signal line loss.
The loss of a T-line manifests itself as signal attenuation (or bandwidth reduction) if the
line simply connects two blocks. With a typical loss of less than 0.5 dB/mm at frequencies
of several tens of gigahertz, a microstrip serves this purpose well. On the other hand, if a
T-line acts as an inductive load whoseQ is critical, then a much lower loss is required.
We can readily relate the loss and theQ. Suppose a T-line of unit length exhibits a series
